_meta.platform: Nintendo Entertainment System (NES) / Famicom Disk System

sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — NES / Metroid
sources.notes: Techniques documentation: morph bomb jumps, bomb boost mechanics, diagonal bomb jumps. Movement descriptions are qualitative — no exact pixel values published.

sources.name: Data Crystal — Metroid
sources.notes: RAM map with velocity addresses ($0020 X velocity, $0022 Y velocity). Physics variable values not fully annotated in public documentation.

sources.name: Metroid Speedrunning Wiki
sources.notes: Technique descriptions for bomb jumping, morph mechanics. Frame-precise mechanics documented qualitatively.

notes: WARNING: Exact ROM-level physics constants for NES Metroid are less thoroughly documented than SMB or Mega Man.
NES Metroid uses room-based scrolling, NOT continuous scrolling. This is architecturally significant.
The 'floaty' feel comes from genuinely lower gravity than contemporaries — not an illusion.
High-Jump Boots increase jump height approximately 1.5x.
Space Jump is NOT present in NES Metroid — introduced in Super Metroid (SNES, 1994).
Charge Beam is NOT present in NES Metroid — introduced in Super Metroid.
All physics values below marked with confidence levels. Verify against Data Crystal RAM map.

units.speed: pixels per frame (approximate — exact sub-pixel unit uncertain without verified RAM map)

scrolling_architecture.description: Metroid uses room-based screen transitions rather than smooth scrolling. Each room is one screen. Entering a door triggers a transition to the adjacent room. This is fundamentally different from SMB's continuous scrolling.
scrolling_architecture.significance: Level generation must account for discrete room units, not tile strips. Camera is always locked to the room.

walk_speed.notes: Samus's walk speed is approximately 2 px/frame. She does not have a separate run speed — constant velocity movement similar to Mega Man. VERIFY against Data Crystal RAM map.

acceleration.notes: Appears to be instant velocity (no acceleration ramp). Movement begins at full speed immediately. VERIFY.

initial_y_velocity_approx.notes: Approximate from gameplay observation and community documentation. VERIFY against ROM.

jump.notes: Jump height is variable — holding jump longer produces higher apex. Samus has meaningful air control, unlike Castlevania.

gravity.notes: Metroid gravity is low compared to contemporaries — this is the source of the 'floaty' feel. Approximately 0.25 px/frame². The game deliberately emulates a sci-fi low-gravity environment. VERIFY exact value.

morph_ball.notes: Morph ball horizontal speed matches Samus's walk speed. The morph ball hitbox is significantly smaller — approximately 1 tile × 1 tile vs Samus's 1×2 standing hitbox.
morph_ball.input: Down to morph, Up to unmorph

hitbox.notes: Morph ball fits through 1-tile-tall passages inaccessible to standing Samus.

bombs.notes: Morph ball bombs explode after approximately 60 frames (1 second at 60fps).

bomb_jump.description: Laying a bomb and unmorphing allows Samus to jump on the explosion for extra height. Timing-sensitive.
bomb_jump.source: TASVideos: 'Samus can get extra height using a bomb by laying a bomb and unmorphing, where the bomb will push her upwards, and you can then jump for extra height.'
bomb_jump.extra_height_constraint: Do not press left or right between unmorph and jump — pressing a direction causes Samus to stop on landing and lose momentum.

diagonal_bomb_boost.description: Lay a bomb, unmorph, move very slightly in the desired direction (only a few pixels), then jump after explosion for a high, far jump.
diagonal_bomb_boost.notes: The 'very slightly' horizontal movement before jumping is critical — too much direction input and the height boost is lost.

bombs.notes: Samus can place up to 3 bombs simultaneously.

high_jump_boots.mechanism: increases initial Y velocity at jump
high_jump_boots.notes: High-Jump Boots are a major exploration unlock — they allow accessing previously unreachable areas. The ~1.5x multiplier is from community gameplay analysis. VERIFY exact multiplier against ROM.

standard_beam.direction: horizontal (can aim up or horizontal, NOT diagonally)
standard_beam.notes: Standard beam fires horizontally or upward. Speed approximately 6 px/frame. VERIFY.

ice_beam.notes: Ice Beam allows freezing enemies as platforms — a key exploration mechanic.

wave_beam.notes: Wave Beam travels in a sine-wave pattern, allowing hits around corners.

missile.notes: Missiles are the primary boss-killing tool. Horizontal-only firing. Speed approximately 6 px/frame. VERIFY.

varia_suit.effect: halves damage taken in hot areas (lava, Norfair)
varia_suit.notes: Not a physics change — damage reduction only. But critical for exploration viability.

samus_standing.notes: Approximate. Samus's hitbox is narrower than her sprite. VERIFY exact values.

wall_jump.notes: No wall-jump mechanics in NES Metroid.

level_structure.navigation: Samus enters doors and transitions to adjacent rooms
notes: NES Metroid is one of the earliest 'Metroidvania' games — open world with ability-gated exploration.
Room-based scrolling means no parallax or smooth camera — the world is composed of discrete screen-sized chambers.
Norfair causes continuous damage without Varia Suit — a gating mechanism via gear dependency.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The room-based architecture is a fundamentally different level generation target than SMB-style tile strips. Instead of generating a tile map with camera scroll, you generate a graph of rooms. Each room is one screen.
The floaty gravity is the Metroid feel. Without it, the game feels like generic NES platformer. The gravity value needs to be genuinely low (≈0.25 px/frame²), not just 'a bit lower than Mario'.

Bomb jumping is the hidden skill expression axis — casual players see bombs as combat tools; advanced players see them as a secondary jump mechanic. This duality (obvious use and advanced use) is great design.

The open-world structure means level generation would need to produce a room graph with choke points that require abilities to pass — not a linear level. This is a paradigm shift from platformer levels.
Ice Beam → enemy platforms is a puzzle mechanic: item A enables mechanic B which unlocks area C. This cascading ability unlock is the core Metroid design pattern.
